name: Fujiwara no Hidesato
description: Fujiwara no Hidesato, also known as Tawara Tōda, was a prominent Japanese nobleman and warrior of the Heian period. Renowned for his martial prowess and loyalty to the Imperial court, he is best remembered for his legendary defeat of the giant centipede, Mukade, on Mount Mikami. Hidesato's origins are somewhat shrouded in mystery, but he rose to prominence through his military achievements and service to the Emperor. His victory over Mukade solidified his reputation as a national hero and protector of the realm. According to legend, the Dragon King of Lake Biwa requested Hidesato's aid in vanquishing the monstrous centipede, which was terrorizing the region. Hidesato bravely confronted the creature, utilizing his bow and arrows to pierce its armored body. As a reward for his bravery, the Dragon King bestowed upon him a set of magical treasures, including a never-ending bag of rice, a bolt of silk that never ran out, and a cooking pot that produced endless food. Hidesato's tale is a popular theme in Japanese folklore, art, and literature, embodying themes of courage, loyalty, and the triumph of good over evil. He represents the ideal of the warrior-aristocrat, dedicated to serving his Emperor and protecting his people from supernatural threats. His story continues to inspire and resonate with audiences today, solidifying his place as a legendary figure in Japanese mythology.
subtitle: Centipede slayer and loyal warrior.
achievements: Receiving magical treasures from the Dragon King
Serving the Imperial Court with distinction
Becoming a symbol of courage and loyalty
Protecting the realm from supernatural threats
companions: The Dragon King of Lake Biwa

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Mukade
keyMyths.description: Hidesato is asked by the Dragon King to slay Mukade, a giant centipede terrorizing Lake Biwa. Hidesato shoots the centipede with his bow and arrows, finally killing it and saving the region.

keyMyths.title: The Dragon King's Gifts
keyMyths.description: As a reward for slaying Mukade, the Dragon King gifts Hidesato magical items, including an endless bag of rice, a bolt of silk that never runs out, and a cooking pot that always produces food. These gifts symbolize abundance and prosperity.

keyMyths.title: Service to the Emperor
keyMyths.description: Beyond his monster-slaying exploits, Hidesato is known for his unwavering loyalty and service to the Emperor and the Imperial Court. He embodies the ideal of the samurai warrior-aristocrat.

sources.author: Donald Keene
sources.description: Provides context on Heian period literature and historical background.
